behaviors.description: Plugin manifests missing required fields are validated and rejected before any plugin code executes.
behaviors.justification: The validation logic is deterministic and self-contained. A unit test verifies the validation function rejects invalid manifest objects (missing name, version, or dependencies) without instantiating the full loader. No filesystem, subprocess, or real plugin infrastructure is required—only direct testing of the validation function's contract.
behaviors.test_description: Create invalid manifest objects, each missing one required field (name, version, or dependencies). Call the manifest validator on each. Assert that each throws ValidationError and verify that no plugin code paths are reached by spying on plugin import/execution functions.

behaviors.description: The loader respects plugin dependencies and calls onLoad hooks in dependency order (dependencies loaded first).
behaviors.justification: The real failure is in the full loader pipeline: the dependency graph must be resolved correctly AND hooks must be called in the computed order. Unit testing only the topological sort algorithm would miss the failure if the loader doesn't actually apply that order. The behavior requires multiple plugins with declared dependencies and the full load sequence working together.
behaviors.test_description: Create two plugin files with manifests: plugin-b (no dependencies), plugin-a (depends on plugin-b). Mock onLoad hooks to record call order. Invoke the loader. Assert that plugin-b's onLoad was called before plugin-a's onLoad.

behaviors.description: When two plugins form a circular dependency, the loader detects the cycle, reports it, and loads neither plugin.
behaviors.justification: Cycle detection algorithm could be unit-tested in isolation, but the real failure occurs when the loader detects a cycle but still loads one or both plugins. The integration boundary requires all three steps to work together: parse manifests, build dependency graph, detect cycle, and exclude those plugins from loading.
behaviors.test_description: Create three plugins: plugin-a depends on plugin-b, plugin-b depends on plugin-a (cycle), and plugin-c has no dependencies. Invoke the loader. Assert: (1) cycle is reported in error message or exception, (2) neither plugin-a nor plugin-b's onLoad is called, (3) plugin-c's onLoad is called normally.

behaviors.description: A plugin's onLoad hook is called only after its entry module has been imported and executed, including all import-time side effects.
behaviors.justification: The failure is whether real Node.js module loading actually occurs before the hook. Mocking require() would hide real failures—plugins have import-time side effects (registering globals, connecting resources) that must happen before onLoad. The real module must be imported with real JavaScript execution; mocking defeats the test's purpose.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real plugin file with an import-time side effect (e.g., setting a global variable). Mock the onLoad hook to record when it's called. Load the plugin. Assert that the import-time side effect has occurred before the onLoad hook was invoked (the global is already set at hook invocation time).

behaviors.description: When a plugin's onLoad hook throws an error, the loader continues loading remaining plugins that do not depend on the failed plugin, but skips those that do.
behaviors.justification: Error handling of a single plugin could be unit-tested with mocks, but the real failure involves the dependency graph. The loader must understand which plugins are safe to load (no transitive dependency on the failed one) and which must be skipped. This requires manifests for multiple plugins and full dependency resolution logic.
behaviors.test_description: Create four plugins: plugin-a (no dependencies), plugin-b (depends on plugin-a), plugin-c (no dependencies), plugin-d (depends on plugin-b). Mock plugin-a's onLoad to throw. Load all plugins. Assert: (1) plugin-a's error is caught/reported, (2) plugin-c's onLoad is called (safe), (3) plugin-b's onLoad is NOT called (depends on failed plugin-a), (4) plugin-d's onLoad is NOT called (depends on plugin-b).
